steps.title: Recognize the comma's structural signal
steps.content: The comma after “A government body officially known as the Althing,” signals that the next part must form a complete independent clause—a sentence with a clear subject and verb. This is how introductory phrases connect to main clauses in Standard English.

steps.title: Match the structure to the answer
steps.content:
The blank needs a sentence that identifies and describes the Althing.

Choice D supplies “Iceland's parliament is one of the oldest in the world, first meeting in 930 CE”—a complete clause where “Iceland's parliament” is the subject, “is” is the verb, and the rest provides supporting details.

This maintains grammatical correctness and coherence. Choices A, B, and C each violate Standard English conventions: A creates an impossible subject with “930 CE was,” B creates a comma splice by joining two independent clauses with only a comma, and C produces an awkward appositive structure that confuses the relationship between phrases.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
